teamObjective.explanation: I hold only low off-suit clubs (3-6) and cannot realistically win this opening lead. The best chance for my team is to play the lowest card so my partner may take the trick with a higher card, while I keep my slightly stronger cards for future opportunities.

partnerBelief.summary: My partner (player 2, 3 cards) has not signaled anything yet. Because the hand is small and the game is new, I cannot assert their strength, but there is a reasonable chance they hold a card higher than 3 or a trump to win the trick.

opponentBelief.summary: Opponent player 1 has only 2 cards and is near finishing, so they will try to win or shed efficiently if possible. Player 3 has 3 cards and is also likely to contest a low lead with any higher card or trump. I assume they have higher cards than my clubs but cannot know their exact holdings.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Leading the 3 of clubs is the least committal play. It gives my partner the best chance to overtake with a higher card, while preserving my 4, 5, and 6 for later tricks where they might matter more.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: The 6 is my highest club but still a weak off-suit card; it will almost certainly be beaten and using it leaves me with even weaker cards (3-5) for the rest of the hand.
Playing the 4 instead of the 3 consumes a slightly higher card without meaningfully changing who can beat the trick, so it is strictly worse for preserving hand strength.

risks: Player 1 (2 cards, near finish) may win the trick and continue toward going out.
My partner may be unable to beat even a 3, in which case the opponents gain the lead anyway.
All my cards are low, so I have little ability to suppress opponents if they take control.
riskAssessment.mitigation: Play the lowest card to minimize my own commitment, let partner and opponents contest the trick, and keep my remaining cards for future tricks where a low winner might arise.

notes: Opening lead with no public history; partner and opponent inferences are highly uncertain.
